result:
:::writing
There is a particular kind of quiet that only shows up after something ends. Not silence—silence is clean, almost intentional. This is messier. It lingers in the corners, clings to objects, settles into fabrics. It sounds like a refrigerator humming too loudly, like footsteps that no longer echo back. It’s the quiet that makes you aware of your own breathing, as if your body is trying to fill a space it was never meant to occupy alone.

I noticed it first in the mornings.

Not right away—at first there was relief. The absence of tension can feel like freedom until your nervous system realizes it doesn’t know what to do with peace. You wake up and nothing is wrong. No messages waiting to be decoded, no emotional weather to predict. Just light slipping through the blinds and the faint outline of your life, unchanged except for the absence of someone who used to give it shape.

You make coffee. You always make coffee. Ritual survives where meaning hesitates.

The mug you reach for still has a faint chip on the rim. You remember when it happened, though the memory feels oddly detached, like watching someone else’s home video. You remember laughing about it, how insignificant it seemed. It’s strange how objects outlast the emotions attached to them, like props after the actors have left the stage.

The coffee tastes the same. You check, just in case.

You tell yourself that this is what you wanted. Or needed. Or both. The words blur together. Want and need are difficult to separate when you’re trying to justify a decision that rearranged your life.

Outside, the world is indifferent in the most comforting way. Cars pass. Someone walks a dog that pauses too long at a patch of grass. A delivery truck double-parks with casual authority. Everything continues, not out of cruelty, but because that’s what everything does.

You sit by the window longer than necessary.

There’s a temptation to assign meaning to small things. The way the light hits the floor, the exact timing of a passing siren, the coincidence of hearing a song you haven’t thought about in years. It’s as if your brain is trying to stitch together a narrative, something that explains why things unfolded the way they did. But meaning, when forced, becomes brittle. It cracks under the slightest pressure.

So you stop trying to make it make sense.

Instead, you notice details.

The way your hands look slightly different than they used to—older, maybe, or just more familiar. The way your thoughts arrive less urgently, like they’ve learned they don’t need to shout to be heard. The way time stretches in some places and compresses in others, as if it’s experimenting with new shapes.

You begin to understand that endings are not singular events. They are processes, slow and uneven. There is the obvious ending—the conversation, the decision, the moment of finality. And then there are the smaller endings that follow: the last time you instinctively reach for your phone to tell them something, the last time you expect to see their name appear, the last time a memory catches you off guard instead of passing by like any other thought.

Each one leaves behind a smaller version of that quiet.

It’s not all heavy.

There are moments—brief, almost shy—when something like curiosity slips in. You find yourself wondering what else might fit into the space that’s been cleared. Not in a dramatic, life-altering way. Just small things. A different route home. A new place to sit. A conversation that doesn’t carry the weight of history.

You start to notice how much of your life was shaped around anticipation. Waiting for responses, for reactions, for the next shift in emotional temperature. Without that, time behaves differently. It becomes less about what’s coming next and more about what’s already here.

It’s unsettling. And then, gradually, it isn’t.

One afternoon, without planning to, you rearrange a room. Not entirely—just enough that it feels unfamiliar. The chair moves closer to the window. The lamp shifts a few inches to the left. It’s subtle, but the space breathes differently. You sit down and realize that nothing is where it used to be, and yet everything still works.

You consider that this might be true for more than just furniture.

The quiet doesn’t disappear. It changes texture. It becomes less like an absence and more like a backdrop, something that allows other sounds to exist without being drowned out. You begin to hear things you hadn’t noticed before—your own thoughts, yes, but also the quieter rhythms of your life.

The hum of the refrigerator becomes just a hum again.

One morning, you wake up and don’t immediately notice what’s missing. Not because it’s been replaced, but because it’s no longer the most important thing in the room. You stretch, make coffee, stand by the window. The dog passes again, or maybe it’s a different dog. The light looks similar, but not identical.

You don’t check.

And for a moment—just a moment—the quiet feels less like something that followed an ending and more like something that’s making space for whatever comes next.
:::writing
